metric_value: 60% using or planning shared messaging services within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 companies 'How SOA is Changing IT'; specifically large companies; respondents either using shared messaging services (ESB/registry) or planning implementation within 12 months.

metric_name: Web Services calls to existing apps — adoption
metric_value: 76% have implemented or plan web services calls to existing applications within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms 'How SOA is Changing IT'. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned.

metric_value: 73% implementing or planning applications-related services within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned.

metric_value: 69% currently use or plan business process/workflow services within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned.

metric_value: 65% currently use or plan XML gateways/adapters within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned.

metric_value: 60% implementing or planning shared messaging services (registry or bus) within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned. Same data point as obs-001 but from Table 1 explicitly.

metric_value: 58% implementing or planning governance/management of services within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned.

metric_value: 49% currently use or plan composite applications within 12 months
notes: Survey of 286 firms. Table 1: SOA Technologies in Use or Planned. Lowest adoption in the table — consistent with composite apps being most advanced SOA capability.

metric_value: most of the market is in second wave (services orchestration phase)
notes: Second wave of ESB capabilities (orchestration): composite applications, event-based processing, real-time message content analysis. Most of market is here in late 2005.

metric_value: ESB of the future will interact with/inherit Services Registry, exhibit policy/governance management, and interact with specialized data bus
notes: Aberdeen three-part prediction for ESB evolution: 1) interact with/inherit Services Registry, 2) policy/governance management of SOA infrastructure, 3) interact with specialized data bus/layer.

metric_name: ESB SOA methodology vs. product distinction
metric_value: Adopting SOA is about choosing methodology rather than buying a product; traditional ESB was a finite-task product
notes: As ESBs absorb more SOA constructs their boundaries become less palpable and more part of SOA architectural fabric; business-level definition and integration will trump technically oriented integration.

metric_value: True ESB must religiously adhere to WS-* stack; proprietary middleware wrappers do not qualify
notes: Cape Clear cited as SOA purist position; marketplace has different flavors of ESB; Mougayar acknowledges spectrum.

metric_value: 4 segments: ESB pioneers/SOA-specific; traditional EAI/EII; application/integration platforms; process-centric
notes: Table 2: ESB Functionality Landscape with vendor, strengths, and issues for each segment.

metric_value: Evaluate ESB extensibility; go beyond messaging to orchestration/mediation; include registry/governance; investigate at least one vendor per segment
notes: Aberdeen conclusions for ESB end users: evaluate extensibility; existing ESBs must go beyond data messaging; services orchestration/mediation/complex manipulations key; don't forget services registry, policy/governance, data bus; investigate one vendor per segment.

metric_value: As companies increase quantity of services, demand for more comprehensive ESBs increases commensurably
notes: Closely related adoption levels linked to interdependence between services availability and need for ESB; governance and management aspects grow with service quantity.

metric_value: Strong on BPM but does not offer granularity of services; weak on architectural scalability; marriage with SOA pioneers could be powerful
notes: Process-centric vendors (Axway, IBM WebSphere Process Server, JBoss jBPM, Magic, Metastorm, Vitria) — analysis of strengths and limitations.
